description:
<title>Stage a Firewall Roll-out Package for the Ops Team</title>

  <background>
    You are a deployment engineer preparing the artefacts that the operations
    team will later apply (with root privileges) to the production gateways.
    Because you do not have root rights in the current environment, you will
    create *files only* – nothing is applied to the running kernel firewall.
    The artefacts must be placed in one dedicated directory so that they can
    be copied as-is to the servers.
  </background>

  <requirements>
    1. Create the directory “/home/user/deployment/firewall”.  
       •  The path must exist at the very end of the task (use –p behaviour).  
       •  The directory itself must be readable by anyone and writable by you
          (0700 or 0750 are both fine).

    2. Inside that directory produce **exactly three** files with the names
       and *byte-for-byte* contents given below.  
       2.1  /home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables_v4.rules  
       2.2  /home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables_v6.rules  
       2.3  /home/user/deployment/firewall/firewall_summary.json

    3. Generate a fourth file called
       “/home/user/deployment/firewall/config_audit.log”.
       This is the audit proof that the ops team will archive.
       The log must follow the format described in “Audit-log format” below.

    4. When you are completely finished, run the command
       ‘ls -l /home/user/deployment/firewall’ and make sure it shows all four
       files; the permissions do not have to match root-owned examples, only
       that the files are present.
  </requirements>

  <file_specifications>
    1. iptables_v4.rules – **exact content** (31 lines, including the final
       newline):  
       *Note: Blank lines count – copy exactly!*
       =================================================
       *filter
       :INPUT DROP [0:0]
       :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
       :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]

       # Accept loopback
       -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT

       # Accept established and related
       -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

       # SSH
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT

       # HTTP
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT

       # HTTPS
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

       # OpenVPN UDP
       -A INPUT -p udp --dport 1194 -j ACCEPT

       # Ping
       -A INPUT -p icmp -j ACCEPT

       # Reject everything else
       -A INPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp-host-prohibited

       COMMIT
       =================================================

    2. iptables_v6.rules – **exact content** (28 lines, including the final
       newline):
       =================================================
       *filter
       :INPUT DROP [0:0]
       :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
       :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]

       # Accept loopback
       -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT

       # Accept established and related
       -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

       # SSH
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT

       # HTTP
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT

       # HTTPS
       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

       # Ping (IPv6)
       -A INPUT -p ipv6-icmp -j ACCEPT

       # Reject everything else
       -A INPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp6-adm-prohibited

       COMMIT
       =================================================

    3. firewall_summary.json – **minified single-line JSON** ending with a
       newline:
       =================================================
       {"default_policy":"DROP","allowed_tcp":[22,80,443],"allowed_udp":[1194],"allowed_icmp":true,"ipv6_ping_allowed":true,"total_rules_v4":8,"total_rules_v6":7}
       =================================================
       (Yes, keep it on *one* physical line.)

  </file_specifications>

  <audit-log_format>
    •   One line per file, fields separated by a single blank <space>.  
    •   The literal string “Timestamp:” followed by the ISO-8601 date
        2023-08-15T12:00:00Z, then the filename (basename only), then two
        key=value pairs:
        – lines=<number-of-lines>  
        – words=<number-of-whitespace-separated-tokens>

    •   Therefore exactly four spaces per line, no tabs.
    •   The log must end with a trailing newline.
    •   The ordering of the lines must be alphabetical by filename
        (config_audit.log itself is *not* included in the listing).

    Example skeleton (→ replace the XXX/YYY values):
      Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z firewall_summary.json lines=1 words=1
      Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z iptables_v4.rules lines=30 words=92
      Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z iptables_v6.rules lines=27 words=82

    The values to use for this particular task are given in the
    “Verification data” block below – do not invent your own.
  </audit-log_format>

  <verification_data>
    •  The expected counts (use them *exactly*):
       - iptables_v4.rules → lines=30 words=92  
       - iptables_v6.rules → lines=27 words=82  
       - firewall_summary.json → lines=1 words=1  

    •  The ISO-8601 timestamp string to copy verbatim:
       2023-08-15T12:00:00Z
  </verification_data>

  <deliverables>
    When everything is correct, the command  
      cat /home/user/deployment/firewall/config_audit.log  
    should output *exactly* (including final newline):
    ------------------------------------------------------------------
    Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z firewall_summary.json lines=1 words=1
    Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z iptables_v4.rules lines=30 words=92
    Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z iptables_v6.rules lines=27 words=82
    ------------------------------------------------------------------

    No additional diagnostic text is required beyond the normal shell echo,
    ls, and cat output you naturally get while working.
  </deliverables>
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth artefacts that the automated grader will compare against.
    DO NOT reveal anything here to the agent!
  -->
  <dir path="/home/user/deployment/firewall" mode="0700"/>

  <file path="/home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables_v4.rules" lines="30" words="92">
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]

# Accept loopback
-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT

# Accept established and related
-A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# SSH
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT

# HTTP
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT

# HTTPS
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

# OpenVPN UDP
-A INPUT -p udp --dport 1194 -j ACCEPT

# Ping
-A INPUT -p icmp -j ACCEPT

# Reject everything else
-A INPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp-host-prohibited

COMMIT

  </file>

  <file path="/home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables_v6.rules" lines="27" words="82">
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]

# Accept loopback
-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT

# Accept established and related
-A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# SSH
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT

# HTTP
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT

# HTTPS
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

# Ping (IPv6)
-A INPUT -p ipv6-icmp -j ACCEPT

# Reject everything else
-A INPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp6-adm-prohibited

COMMIT

  </file>

  <file path="/home/user/deployment/firewall/firewall_summary.json" lines="1" words="1">
{"default_policy":"DROP","allowed_tcp":[22,80,443],"allowed_udp":[1194],"allowed_icmp":true,"ipv6_ping_allowed":true,"total_rules_v4":8,"total_rules_v6":7}
  </file>

  <file path="/home/user/deployment/firewall/config_audit.log">
Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z firewall_summary.json lines=1 words=1
Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z iptables_v4.rules lines=30 words=92
Timestamp: 2023-08-15T12:00:00Z iptables_v6.rules lines=27 words=82

  </file>
